sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=32, col=20).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 98, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 26 of about 33 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 9 actions returned the board to a state already seen 9 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 9 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
GAME_OVER again! The chain moved:
- Orange: (17-21, 25-29) → (18-22, 23-27) - moved down 1, left 2
- White: (26-30, 22-26) → (30-34, 18-22) - moved down 4, left 4
- Water: split into two parts: 107 + 38 = 145 pixels (was 146)
- Timer: 57 → 55 pixels
The chain moved left and down, away from the orange ring at (48-54, 37-43). This caused GAME_OVER.
So clicking water at (32, 20) pulled the chain left and down, which was the wrong direction. The chain needs to move right and down toward the ring.
Let me reconsider the mechanics:
- Clicking water below the chain pulls it down
- Clicking water to the left of the chain pulls it left
- Clicking water to the right of the chain pulls it right
The chain is now at orange (18-22, 23-27) and white (30-34, 18-22). The ring is at (48-54, 37-43). The chain needs to move right and down.
But the game is over. I need to click the orange ring to reset the state to NOT_FINISHED, like I did before.
Actually, wait. Looking at the previous pattern, when the game was over, clicking the orange ring changed the state from GAME_OVER to NOT_FINISHED and reset the chain position. Let me do that again.
But first, let me note the key insight:
- The chain moves toward the clicked water position
- Clicking water below pulls the chain down
- Clicking water to the left pulls the chain left
- Clicking water to the right pulls the chain right
- If the chain moves too far from the ring, it's GAME_OVER
So to solve the level, I need to guide the chain to the orange ring by clicking water in the direction I want the chain to move. The chain should end up at or near the ring.
Currently, the chain is at (18-22, 23-27) for orange and (30-34, 18-22) for white. The ring is at (48-54, 37-43). The chain needs to move right and down.
But the game is over, so I need to reset first by clicking the ring.
Actually, looking more carefully at the previous successful moves:
- Clicking (22, 24) pulled the chain down and right
- Clicking (25, 24) pulled the chain down
- Clicking (28, 24) pulled the chain down
- Clicking (31, 28) did nothing
- Clicking (31, 24) did nothing
- Clicking (30, 30) did nothing
- Clicking (32, 20) pulled the chain left and down (GAME_OVER)
The chain moved successfully when I clicked water directly below it (col 24). When I clicked water to the side, it either didn't move or moved in the wrong direction.
The issue is that the chain is now stuck because the water around it is no longer in the right configuration. Or maybe the chain can only move a limited distance.
Let me reset by clicking the ring and try a different approach. Maybe I need to click the ring when the chain is at the right position, not try to move the chain all the way to the ring.
Actually, looking at the level again, maybe the goal is not to move the chain to the ring, but to click the ring when the chain is in a specific configuration. Or maybe the goal is to move the chain to touch the ring.
Let me reset and try clicking the ring when the chain is close enough.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: GAME_OVER! Chain moved left and down, away from ring. Need to reset by clicking the orange ring, then try a different approach.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Reset by clicking the orange ring
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 51, 'col': 40}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== After clicking ring ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'G', 'O', 'p', 'S', 'M'] and n['pixels'] > 5:
        rows = [b[0] for b in n['boundary']]
        cols = [b[1] for b in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
print("State:", last_action_result.get('state'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

G: pixels=12, rows=19-23, cols=6-10

S: pixels=280, rows=32-54, cols=1-28

p: pixels=20, rows=39-43, cols=47-51
G: pixels=12, rows=46-50, cols=52-56

Game over: False
